description: Benchmark evals for the Noir ZK Privacy skill. Run each prompt against Claude WITHOUT the skill loaded, grade pass/fail using the rubric. Any eval that passes baseline = that content may be redundant in the skill.

instructions.step_1: For each eval, send the prompt to Claude in a fresh conversation with NO skill loaded.
instructions.step_2: Grade the response pass/fail using the rubric. Be strict — partial credit is a fail.
instructions.step_4: Repeat with the skill loaded (paste noir/SKILL.md into context first). Record in 'with_skill' column.
instructions.step_5: Compare. Baseline pass + skill pass = content is redundant. Baseline fail + skill pass = content is valuable. Baseline fail + skill fail = content needs improvement.

evals.claim: nargo prove/verify were removed; must use bb CLI directly
evals.prompt: Show me the step-by-step commands to compile a Noir circuit, generate a proof, and verify it. Start from a project that already has src/main.nr written.

pass_criteria: Uses 'nargo compile' for compilation
Uses 'nargo execute' (NOT 'nargo prove') for witness generation
Uses 'bb prove' (NOT 'nargo prove') for proof generation
Uses 'bb verify' (NOT 'nargo verify') for verification
Includes correct flags (-b, -w, -o for bb commands)
fail_signals: Uses snarkjs or other non-Barretenberg backend commands

evals.baseline: FAIL — used nargo prove, nargo verify, nargo codegen-verifier (all removed)

evals.claim: Poseidon ~600 gates vs SHA256 ~30,000 gates; correct import is std::hash::poseidon::bn254::hash_2
evals.prompt: I'm building a ZK circuit in Noir that needs to hash values for a commitment scheme. Which hash function should I use and why? Show me the Noir code.

pass_criteria: Recommends Poseidon (not SHA256 or Keccak) for in-circuit hashing
Mentions gate cost difference or circuit efficiency as the reason
Uses std::hash::poseidon::bn254 module path (not a made-up import)
Uses hash_1 or hash_2 function (not a generic 'poseidon()' call)
fail_signals: Recommends SHA256 or Keccak for in-circuit use
Calls a nonexistent function like Poseidon2::hash([...], 2)
No mention of why Poseidon over SHA256

evals.baseline: FAIL — right concept (Poseidon), wrong API (Poseidon2::hash instead of hash_2, wrong import path)

evals.claim: --oracle_hash keccak flag is required for EVM-compatible Solidity verifier
evals.prompt: How do I generate a Solidity verifier contract from my compiled Noir circuit so I can verify proofs onchain?

pass_criteria: Uses 'bb write_vk' to generate verification key
Uses 'bb write_solidity_verifier' (not 'bb contract', not nargo, not snarkjs)
Includes '--oracle_hash keccak' flag on write_vk
Explains that keccak is needed for EVM compatibility

evals.baseline: FAIL — used 'bb contract' (wrong), missing --oracle_hash keccak entirely

evals.claim: LLMs can't write correct Noir circuit code for the commitment-nullifier pattern
evals.prompt: Write a complete Noir circuit (main.nr) for an anonymous voting system. The circuit should prove: the voter knows a secret that corresponds to a registered commitment in a Merkle tree, and output a nullifier hash to prevent double voting. Include the Merkle proof verification.

pass_criteria: fn main() with correct private inputs (nullifier, secret, merkle_path, merkle_indices)
Public inputs marked with 'pub' (merkle_root, nullifier_hash)
Uses std::hash::poseidon::bn254::hash_2 for hashing
Recomputes commitment from nullifier + secret
Verifies Merkle proof against public root
Computes and asserts nullifier_hash
Merkle tree traversal logic is correct (left/right based on index bits)
fail_signals: Uses nonexistent methods like .lt() on Field

evals.baseline: PARTIAL FAIL — good structure, but uses pedersen_hash (wrong), fake .lt() method on Field

evals.prompt: Show me how to generate a Noir ZK proof in the browser using JavaScript/TypeScript. Include the imports, initialization, and proof generation flow.

pass_criteria: Imports from '@noir-lang/noir_js' (Noir class)
Imports from '@aztec/bb.js' (UltraHonkBackend)
Creates UltraHonkBackend with circuit bytecode
Uses noir.execute(inputs) to generate witness
Uses backend.generateProof(witness) to create proof
Proof result has .proof (Uint8Array) and .publicInputs
fail_signals: Uses UltraPlonkBackend as primary (old class name)

evals.claim: Noir proofs work on most EVM chains but NOT zkSync ERA or Polygon zkEVM
evals.prompt: I want to deploy a Noir-based privacy app. Which EVM chains can I deploy the Solidity verifier to? Are there any chains where it won't work?

pass_criteria: Lists mainstream EVM chains as compatible (Ethereum, Optimism, Arbitrum, Base)
Identifies zkSync ERA as incompatible (different precompile addresses)
Mentions BN254 precompiles (ecAdd/ecMul/ecPairing) as the requirement
Warns about Polygon zkEVM (incompatible or being shut down)
fail_signals: Says all EVM chains are compatible

evals.baseline: PARTIAL PASS — gets zkSync right, knows precompiles, but says Polygon zkEVM works (it's being shut down)

removed_evals_baseline_passed.action: Removed dedicated section from skill. Concept still demonstrated in circuit code examples.
Removed conceptual explanation (How It Works). Kept circuit implementation as the reference.
Kept Solidity contract (correct reference code) but trimmed surrounding explanation.
Removed entirely. Baseline gives an excellent explanation of the ZK mental model.
Kept as checklist item only. Removed dedicated explanation.
Removed entirely. Baseline correctly distinguishes ACIR/R1CS, backends, syntax.
Removed entirely. Baseline correctly explains Field limitations and integer alternatives.

scoring.baseline_pass_and_skill_pass: REDUNDANT — the base model already knows this. Consider removing from skill.
scoring.baseline_fail_and_skill_pass: VALUABLE — this is exactly what the skill should contain. Keep it.
scoring.baseline_fail_and_skill_fail: BROKEN — the skill teaches this but doesn't fix the model's behavior. Rewrite this section.
scoring.baseline_pass_and_skill_fail: REGRESSION — the skill somehow makes things worse. Investigate immediately.

summary: 5 FAIL, 1 PARTIAL FAIL, 1 PARTIAL PASS, 6 PASS out of 13 evals. Skill trimmed from 399 to 310 lines. Removed 7 sections where baseline passes. Retained 6 evals targeting version-specific APIs where baseline fails.
